  • Tutorial: CO₂ Enrichment
  • Tutorial: CO₂ Enrichment

Tutorial: CO₂ Enrichment

Why CO₂ matters

Ambient CO₂ (~420 ppm) limits photosynthesis. Enriching to 800–1000 ppm increases net assimilation by 20–40% for C₃ crops like tomato and lettuce (Nederhoff 1994). However, CO₂ injected with vents open escapes immediately, so enrichment strategy interacts with ventilation control.

CO₂ mass balance

GreenhousesJL tracks CO₂ concentration in the air volume [mg/m³]:

\[\frac{dC_{CO_2}}{dt} = \frac{\dot{m}_{inj} - \dot{m}_{vent} - \dot{m}_{can}}{h_{air}}\]

where:

  • ṁ_inj — injection rate [mg/(m²·s)]
  • ṁ_vent = f_vent × (C_air − C_out) — ventilation loss
  • ṁ_can = Aₙ × 44.01×10⁻³ — canopy uptake from Farquhar photosynthesis
  • h_air — air volume height [m]

Conversion: 1 ppm = 1.94 mg/m³ at STP.

Three control modes

1. No enrichment (default)

p = GreenhouseParams(tmy; co2=NoCO2())

CO₂ is tracked but not injected. Daytime photosynthesis depletes CO₂ below ambient; ventilation brings it back toward 420 ppm.

2. On/off enrichment

p = GreenhouseParams(tmy; co2=CO2Enrichment(target=800.0, rate=7.0))

Injects at maximum rate (7 mg/(m²·s)) until target is reached, then stops. Only active when roof vents are mostly closed (U_roof < 0.3).

ParameterDefaultRangeUnit
target800600–1200ppm
rate7.03–15mg/(m²·s)

For proportional control instead of on/off:

p = GreenhouseParams(tmy;
    co2=CO2Config(CO2_target=900.0, injection_max=10.0, enrichment=:proportional))

3. Economic optimal enrichment (Pontryagin-inspired)

p = GreenhouseParams(tmy; co2=EconomicCO2())

Maximises the instantaneous Hamiltonian:

\[H = w_{crop} \times PAR_{factor} \times A_n(CO_2) - w_{co2} \times u_c - w_{vent} \times f_{vent} \times CO_2\]

The stationarity condition ∂H/∂uₐ = 0 gives: inject when the marginal photosynthetic value of CO₂ exceeds its marginal cost. The marginal response is the Farquhar Michaelis-Menten derivative:

\[\frac{dA_n}{dCO_2} = \frac{K_m + \Gamma^*}{(CO_2 + K_m)^2}\]

This produces physically-grounded injection schedules:

  • High PAR, low CO₂ → strong injection (high marginal return)
  • High PAR, high CO₂ → weak injection (diminishing returns)
  • Night or cloudy → no injection (zero return)
  • Vents open → reduced injection (ventilation losses dominate)
ParameterDefaultMeaning
injection_max7.0Max injection [mg/(m²·s)]
w_crop500.0Marginal value of assimilation
w_co20.1Marginal cost of CO₂ supply
w_vent_loss50.0Ventilation loss penalty
Km_ppm600Half-saturation [ppm]
Gamma_ppm50CO₂ compensation point [ppm]

Custom weights:

p = GreenhouseParams(tmy;
    co2=EconomicCO2(w_crop=800.0, w_co2=0.2, injection_max=10.0))
Placeholder weights

The default weights produce reasonable behaviour (day mean ~700 ppm at full sun, no injection at night) but need calibration against experimental data or economic parameters (crop price, CO₂ cost per kg).

Comparing modes

Typical 7-day Brussels winter simulation results:

ModeDay mean CO₂Night mean CO₂Behaviour
NoCO2()~95 ppm~420 ppmDepleted by photosynthesis
CO2Enrichment(target=800)800 ppm800 ppmConstant, wastes CO₂ at night
EconomicCO2()~700 ppm~350 ppmLight-responsive, no night waste

References

  • van Henten, E.J. (1994). Greenhouse climate management: an optimal control approach. PhD, Wageningen.
  • Tap, R.F. (2000). Economics-based optimal control of greenhouse tomato crop production. PhD, Wageningen.
  • Seginer, I., Gary, C. & Tchamitchian, M. (1994). Optimal temperature regimes for a greenhouse crop with a carbohydrate pool. Scientia Horticulturae, 60, 55–80.
  • van Straten, G. et al. (2011). Optimal Control of Greenhouse Cultivation. CRC Press.
  • Nederhoff, E.M. (1994). Effects of CO₂ concentration on photosynthesis, transpiration and production of greenhouse fruit vegetables. PhD, Wageningen.
